description: Golden dataset for evaluating Claude's ability to judge extraction boundaries in the Spring Music monolith. Each example is a proposed extraction plan; the label is the ground truth from ADR-001-decomposition.md.

label_schema.CORRECT: This boundary is a good extraction candidate — cohesive, low coupling, clean API contract
label_schema.INCORRECT: This boundary would be a poor extraction — splits cohesion, leaves dangling deps, or exposes internals

difficulty_schema.easy: Clear-cut case, a well-reasoned model should get this right at HIGH confidence
difficulty_schema.medium: Requires understanding of coupling and data ownership
difficulty_schema.hard: Boundary case — plausible but wrong, or correct for non-obvious reasons

examples.label: CORRECT
examples.title: Full Album Catalog Service
examples.description: Extract AlbumController, Album domain entity, RandomIdGenerator, all repository implementations (JpaAlbumRepository, MongoAlbumRepository, RedisAlbumRepository), and AlbumRepositoryPopulator as a standalone Album Catalog Service with its own persistence.
examples.ground_truth_reasoning: This is the canonical correct extraction from ADR-001 Seam 2+3. Includes the full business capability with all its persistence options and seed data logic. Clean REST boundary, no dangling dependencies.

examples.label: INCORRECT
examples.title: Redis Repository Alone
examples.description: Extract only RedisAlbumRepository and RedisConfig as a standalone 'Redis Storage Service', leaving AlbumController and other repositories in the monolith.
examples.ground_truth_reasoning: A repository implementation is not a deployable service — it has no API boundary. Extracting a single persistence adapter leaves AlbumController with a broken dependency graph. This splits infrastructure from the business capability that owns it.

examples.label: INCORRECT
examples.title: Controller Without Repositories
examples.description: Extract only AlbumController as a standalone 'Album API Service', keeping all repository implementations in the monolith and communicating via remote calls.
examples.ground_truth_reasoning: AlbumController's entire purpose is to delegate to CrudRepository. Separating them without a shared contract or API between them creates a distributed call for every request and moves zero business logic out of the monolith.

examples.label: INCORRECT
examples.title: Error and Info Endpoints as Monitoring Service
examples.description: Extract ErrorController and InfoController as a standalone 'Monitoring Service' alongside the main application.
examples.ground_truth_reasoning: ErrorController contains dangerous unauthenticated chaos endpoints (kill, fill-heap) that should be deleted, not extracted. InfoController reads Cloud Foundry environment state — extracting it separately adds a remote call for health information with no benefit.

examples.label: CORRECT
examples.title: Album Catalog with JPA Only (PostgreSQL default)
examples.description: Extract AlbumController, Album, RandomIdGenerator, JpaAlbumRepository, and AlbumRepositoryPopulator as a standalone service using PostgreSQL as the only persistence backend. Drop Mongo and Redis support in the extracted service.
examples.ground_truth_reasoning: This is the recommended approach in ADR-001: PostgreSQL as default, drop Cloud Foundry multi-store complexity in the extracted service. Cohesive, testable, clean boundary. Dropping Mongo/Redis support is a deliberate simplification consistent with the modernization goal.

examples.label: INCORRECT
examples.title: Album Catalog Without Seed Data
examples.description: Extract AlbumController, Album, RandomIdGenerator, and all repositories — but leave AlbumRepositoryPopulator in the monolith as a 'data migration concern'.
examples.ground_truth_reasoning: ADR-001 Seam 3 explicitly states AlbumRepositoryPopulator must move WITH the Catalog Service. The rule 'populate on first startup if empty' is a business rule owned by the catalog, not an infrastructure concern. Leaving it in the monolith creates a cross-service dependency for initialization.

examples.label: INCORRECT
examples.title: SpringApplicationContextInitializer as Config Service
examples.description: Extract SpringApplicationContextInitializer and RedisConfig as a standalone 'Configuration Service' that the main application calls at startup to determine its active profile.
examples.ground_truth_reasoning: Context initialization is not a deployable service — it runs in-process during Spring application startup. Turning it into a remote service creates a distributed startup dependency and defeats the purpose of Spring's context initialization mechanism entirely.

examples.label: INCORRECT
examples.title: Domain Model Alone as Shared Library
examples.description: Extract Album, RandomIdGenerator, and ApplicationInfo as a standalone 'domain model library' jar shared between the monolith and future extracted services.
examples.ground_truth_reasoning: Extracting the domain model as a shared library creates a tight coupling between services via a shared JAR. Changes to Album's fields would require redeployment of all consumers. The domain model should be owned by and bundled inside the Album Catalog Service, not shared.

examples.label: INCORRECT
examples.title: Album Catalog Exposing albumId Field in API
examples.description: Extract the full Album Catalog Service (AlbumController + all repos + Album entity) but keep the current Album JSON shape in the external API, including the internal 'albumId' field alongside the generated 'id' field.
examples.ground_truth_reasoning: The extraction boundary is correct, but exposing 'albumId' in the new service's API leaks an internal identifier from the monolith's data model. The anti-corruption layer principle (ADR-001 Seam 2) requires that the new service's API contract be clean. This is The Fence violation.

examples.label: CORRECT
examples.title: Delete ErrorController, Restrict Actuator
examples.description: Rather than extracting ErrorController, delete it entirely and restrict Spring Actuator to expose only 'health' and 'info' endpoints. This is the first change before any service extraction.
examples.ground_truth_reasoning: ADR-001 Seam 1 explicitly calls this a deletion not an extraction. ErrorController contains unauthenticated chaos endpoints — deleting them reduces attack surface. This is the correct first action before any extraction, even though it's not an extraction itself.

examples.label: INCORRECT
examples.title: AlbumRepositoryPopulator as Standalone Seeding Service
examples.description: Extract AlbumRepositoryPopulator as a standalone 'Data Seeding Service' that runs once at startup and populates the album catalog via the Album Catalog Service's REST API.
examples.ground_truth_reasoning: While superficially appealing, turning seed data logic into a separate service adds network dependency to initialization, introduces a race condition (seeder must wait for catalog to be ready), and creates operational complexity for what is essentially a startup side-effect. ADR-001 says move it with the catalog, not separate it.

examples.label: INCORRECT
examples.title: Full Catalog Keeping Cloud Foundry Wiring
examples.description: Extract the full Album Catalog Service (AlbumController + all repos + domain) but keep SpringApplicationContextInitializer inside the extracted service to preserve Cloud Foundry VCAP_SERVICES-based profile detection.
examples.ground_truth_reasoning: The business capability extraction is correct, but keeping Cloud Foundry wiring defeats the modernization goal. The extracted service should use standard environment variables or Spring Cloud Config, not VCAP_SERVICES. Carrying the CF dependency forward is explicitly listed in ADR-001 as something NOT to do.
